raw_case:
Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia in an Asian Male: A Case Report
Penile intraepithelial neoplasia is a rare presentation in Asian males. It is usually observed as small lesions in the penis. It primarily influences the penetrative sexual intercourse ability and urination. Major risk factors associated are smoking, human papillomavirus infection, phimosis, tobacco consumption, and poor hygiene practices. Prompt diagnosis and treatment can lead to good prognostic outcomes. A 70-year-old male presented with penile swelling, weight loss, and burning micturition. The lesion on initial presentation looked malignant clinically due to the abnormal size of the lesion. This was later found to be premalignant/benign on histopathology. The lesion was timely treated saving the patient from losing organ functionality and from the terrifying effects of chemotherapy.

Introduction

Penile intraepithelial neoplasia is a rare precancerous condition affecting the outer skin layer of the penis, primarily the glans and foreskin, majorly affecting urination and penetrative sexual intercourse [1]. It is characterized by abnormal cell changes that can potentially progress to invasive squamous cell carcinoma if left untreated [2]. Penile intraepithelial neoplasia is often classified into subtypes, including undifferentiated and differentiated forms, with the undifferentiated type being more common [3]. It is considered a precursor to penile cancer and has shown a slight increase in incidence from 0.85/100,000 person-years in 1997-1998 to 1.13/100,000 person-years in 2017-2018 [4]. It is rare but can sometimes be observed in Asian males between 60 and 70 years [5]. It typically presents as single or multiple lesions that may appear as red plaques on the glans or inner foreskin with different textures [2]. Symptoms may include painful urination, discharge, and difficulty retracting the foreskin. Treatment options vary based on the severity and recurrence of the lesions. Surgical intervention, particularly circumcision, is often the primary treatment, as topical treatments alone have shown limited effectiveness. Mohs micrographic surgery is also considered effective for severe cases [1,6]. This is a case of a 70-year-old male with a primary complaint of penile swelling for six months. The diagnosis was later confirmed to be penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3 on histopathological analysis.

Case presentation

A male patient of 70 years presented with a complaint of ulceroproliferative growth over the penile region for the past six months. The patient did not have any other medical conditions before six months. The swelling onset was insidious, gradually progressing to its current size of 3 x 3 cm and accompanied by pain. The patient also has a history of urinary hesitancy, burning micturition, and self-reported weight loss (measured by loosening of clothes). There was no noted history of nocturia, bowel complaints, fever, cough, and lower limb swelling. There were no associated comorbidities noted. Local examination showed a solitary ulceroproliferative growth of 3 x 3 cm over the penile prepuce extending up to the glans. The lump had an irregular surface and ill-defined margins without active bleeding or discharge. External meatus was normal. Penile edema was present, with no rise in local temperature. A 1 x 1 cm firm, smooth mobile lymph node palpable in the right inguinal region was observed with bilateral testis palpable (Figure 1).Ultrasound imaging (USG) of the inguinoscrotal region was suggestive of a right-sided inguinal hernia and a right-sided edematous spermatic cord with raised vascularity, with a probable diagnosis of funiculitis, bilateral hydrocele (right > left), and inguinal lymphadenopathy (Figure 2).USG of the abdomen pelvis was found to be normal. The patient was further subjected to wedge biopsy from the ulceroproliferative growth over prepuce. The histopathological examination of the same was suggestive of penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3 (Figure 3).Based on the final diagnosis of penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3, the patient was planned for wide local excision of penile tumor under spinal anesthesia. The excised specimen was sent for frozen section analysis, which was suggestive carcinoma in situ with negative margins. Hence, penectomy was not done, and only excision of growth was carried out with reconstruction for cosmetic satisfaction (Figures 4-6).The excised specimen was sent for histopathological evaluation, which was suggestive of penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3, and margins were negative. The smears showed a well-distributed folliculated population of polymorphous lymphoid cells. It was composed of follicular center cells, small lymphocytes, and immunoblasts with few histiocytes and plasmacytoid lymphocytes without any malignant cells (Figures 7, 8).On histopathological analysis, tissue sections from the proximal and distal margins were negative for infiltration by malignant cells. The patient came for follow-up after 15 days. The suture line was healthy. Further management was done as per the advice of a medical oncologist. The patient was advised to apply 5-fluorouracil locally for one month.

Discussion

Penile intraepithelial neoplasia is rare in Asian males. Other locations for intraepithelial neoplasia are the neck, hands, trunk, oral cavity, head, buttocks, and nail bed, with a rare observation in the penis [5]. Penile intraepithelial neoplasia is a precancerous penile dermatosis where the epithelium displays dysplastic changes with an intact basement membrane. It displays atypical squamous cells with variations in size and shape, nuclear atypia, and increased mitotic activity. However, these changes are confined only to the epithelial layer compared to penile cancer, which is an invasive malignancy. Penile intraepithelial neoplasia may develop into cancer if not timely addressed [5,6]. Diagnosis can be carried out with the help of radiological assessment tools such as ultrasound, computed tomography, positron emission tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging along with physical presentation. However, a corresponding histopathological analysis is required to confirm the diagnosis [6]. Penile intraepithelial neoplasia was reported between 4% and 25% depending on the management strategies, viz. wide local incision, total glans resurfacing, glansectomy, and Mohs surgery [7]. Procedures that spare organs and glands can be used to treat low-risk cancers. If untreated, approximately 10%-30% of the penile intraepithelial neoplasia cases may progress to invasive squamous cell carcinoma, highlighting the importance of early detection and management [6,7]. Regular follow-up is essential due to the potential for recurrence after surgical treatment in 15%-20% of patients [8]. Though penile intraepithelial neoplasia is known to be common in males above 60 years of age, there are cases of age as young as 22 years male affected by the disease, which creates a need to emphasize the importance of reporting more cases [2,5]. Treatment of penile intraepithelial neoplasia generally requires surgical excision of the affected areas to prevent progression to invasive cancer and topical therapy, compared to penile cancers, which require a more vigorous multidisciplinary approach for its treatment [6,7]. Also, there is evidence of lower incidence of penile intraepithelial neoplasia in males undergoing circumcision. Though the underlying mechanisms are not clearly understood, it has been linked to lowered rates of human papillomavirus and other infections, reducing the chances of penile intraepithelial neoplasia [9]. This patient had a similar clinical presentation of a lesion with no urinary complaints, but unusual growth of the lesion. This clinical presentation was suggestive of malignancy, which was ruled out as a diagnosis of penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3 by histopathological analysis. Timely diagnosis and intervention can be counted as helpful in achieving a good outcome, maintaining penile function, achieving a satisfactory quality of life, and in the prevention of the development of invasive penile cancer.

Conclusions

Though a rarity in Asian males with a common age of presentation of greater than 60 years, penile intraepithelial neoplasia is documented in younger males, too. Additional research must be carried out reporting clinical presentations and pathophysiology, which can aid early diagnosis and prompt treatment. This can provide crucial support in organ function preservation and significantly improve the physical and emotional quality of life of the patients.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 70-year-old Asian male.
- Chief Complaint: Penile swelling.
- History of Present Illness: Gradual onset of swelling in the penile region over six months, progressing to a 3x3 cm ulceroproliferative lesion. Symptoms included burning micturition, urinary hesitancy, and self-reported weight loss. No fever, nocturia, bowel complaints, cough, or lower limb swelling were noted.
- Past Medical History: None reported within the preceding six months.
- Personal History: Not provided.
- Family History: Not provided.
- Physical Examination: Solitary 3x3 cm ulceroproliferative lesion over the penile prepuce extending to the glans. No active bleeding or discharge. Normal external meatus. Penile edema and a right inguinal lymph node (firm, smooth, mobile, 1x1 cm) observed. Bilateral testis palpable.
- Ancillary Tests:  
  - Ultrasound (USG) revealed a right-sided inguinal hernia, edematous spermatic cord with raised vascularity (suggestive of funiculitis), bilateral hydrocele, and inguinal lymphadenopathy. Abdominal and pelvic USG were unremarkable.
  - Wedge biopsy of the lesion confirmed penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3.
  - Histopathological examination of excised tissue post-surgery confirmed carcinoma in situ with negative margins.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations**: The ulceroproliferative lesion's morphology, associated penile edema, and lymphadenopathy raised suspicion for malignancy (e.g., invasive squamous cell carcinoma). The patient's age and symptoms (burning micturition, weight loss) supported this concern.
2. **Diagnostic Exclusions**:  
   - **Invasive penile cancer**: Histopathology ruled this out, showing changes confined to the epithelium without malignancy in deeper tissues.  
   - **Infectious causes**: Absence of fever, discharge, or systemic signs reduced the likelihood of infections such as syphilis, balanitis, or fungal infections.  
   - **Other penile dermatoses**: Clinical appearance and histopathological findings excluded conditions like lichen planus and psoriasis of the penile skin.
3. **Confirmation**: Wedge biopsy identified penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3, a high-grade precancerous condition. Histopathology of excised tissue further confirmed carcinoma in situ with negative surgical margins, ruling out invasive carcinoma.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3, a high-grade precancerous epithelial lesion confined to the outer layer of the skin with an intact basement membrane.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let me try to work through this case. So, the patient is a 70-year-old Asian male with penile swelling that's been getting worse over six months. The main issues he's had are burning when urinating, difficulty starting to urinate, and some weight loss. The lesion is described as ulceroproliferative, 3x3 cm, involving the prepuce and glans. There's also penile edema and a right inguinal lymph node that's firm and mobile. 

First, I need to think about the possible causes of a penile lesion. The key things here are the chronic progression and the biopsy results. The biopsy showed penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3, which is a high-grade precancerous lesion. Then after surgery, the histopathology confirmed carcinoma in situ with negative margins. Carcinoma in situ is considered an early form of cancer where the cells are malignant but haven't invaded deeper tissues. 

But wait, even though the excised tissue has negative margins, there's the presence of an inguinal lymph node. Inguinal lymphadenopathy can be reactive or metastatic. Given the primary lesion on the penis, metastatic spread is a concern. However, the lymph node here is described as 1x1 cm, firm, smooth, and mobile. Typically, metastatic nodes might be harder, fixed, or larger, but not always. The ultrasound showed inguinal lymphadenopathy, but the biopsy of the lesion was grade 3 neoplasia, which is pre-invasive. Carcinoma in situ by definition hasn't invaded past the basement membrane, so metastasis shouldn't occur. But sometimes, carcinoma in situ can be associated with invasive components if not entirely sampled.

Wait, but the histopathology post-surgery says carcinoma in situ with negative margins. So if the margins are clear, maybe there's no invasion. But then why is there lymphadenopathy? Maybe the lymph node is reactive due to infection or inflammation. The ultrasound also mentioned funiculitis (inflammation of the spermatic cord) and a hydrocele, which can be related to infection. The patient had burning micturition, which could indicate a urinary tract infection, but there's no mention of fever or other systemic symptoms. 

Another angle: Penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3 (PeIN III) is synonymous with squamous cell carcinoma in situ. So the diagnosis here would be squamous cell carcinoma in situ of the penis. However, the presence of a lymph node could suggest progression to invasive carcinoma, but the histopathology didn't show invasion. Maybe the initial biopsy was grade 3, but the surgical excision confirmed it's still in situ. 

But in some cases, carcinoma in situ can be associated with a higher risk of developing invasive cancer if not treated. Since the patient underwent surgery with clear margins, the primary lesion is addressed. The lymphadenopathy might need further evaluation. However, based on the provided information, the final diagnosis after surgery is carcinoma in situ with negative margins. The right inguinal lymph node might need a biopsy to rule out metastasis, but that's not part of the current data. 

So putting it all together: the key findings are the biopsy showing PeIN III, and post-surgical histology confirming carcinoma in situ. Therefore, the final diagnosis is Penile Squamous Cell Carcinoma in situ. The other findings like lymphadenopathy and funiculitis are likely reactive or secondary, but not indicative of invasive cancer based on current data.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a chronic, progressive penile ulceroproliferative lesion, urinary symptoms, and weight loss. The lesion's persistence and characteristics (ulceroproliferative, penile edema) raise suspicion for malignancy, particularly squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), which is common in older males and linked to risk factors like poor hygiene or HPV infection. 

<step 2> The biopsy showing penile intraepithelial neoplasia (PeIN) grade 3 indicates high-grade dysplasia/carcinoma in situ. Post-excision histopathology confirming carcinoma in situ with negative margins supports this, as PeIN III is a precursor to invasive SCC but remains confined to the epithelium. 

<step 3> The right inguinal lymphadenopathy could suggest metastasis, but the node's small size, mobility, and smoothness are atypical for metastatic SCC. Reactive lymphadenopathy secondary to inflammation (e.g., funiculitis or hydrocele noted on USG) is more likely, given the absence of histologic evidence of invasive carcinoma in the excised specimen.

<step 4> Other findings (inguinal hernia, hydrocele, funiculitis) are incidental and unrelated to the primary diagnosis. The absence of systemic symptoms (fever, cough) and normal abdominal/pelvic imaging further narrows the focus to localized penile pathology.

### Answer:
Penile squamous cell carcinoma in situ.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with a chronic, progressive penile ulceroproliferative lesion, urinary symptoms, and weight loss. The lesion's persistence and characteristics (ulceroproliferative, penile edema) raise suspicion for malignancy, particularly squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), which is common in older males and linked to risk factors like poor hygiene or HPV infection.

The biopsy showing penile intraepithelial neoplasia (PeIN) grade 3 indicates high-grade dysplasia/carcinoma in situ. Post-excision histopathology confirming carcinoma in situ with negative margins supports this, as PeIN III is a precursor to invasive SCC but remains confined to the epithelium.

The right inguinal lymphadenopathy could suggest metastasis, but the node's small size, mobility, and smoothness are atypical for metastatic SCC. Reactive lymphadenopathy secondary to inflammation (e.g., funiculitis or hydrocele noted on USG) is more likely, given the absence of histologic evidence of invasive carcinoma in the excised specimen.

Other findings (inguinal hernia, hydrocele, funiculitis) are incidental and unrelated to the primary diagnosis. The absence of systemic symptoms (fever, cough) and normal abdominal/pelvic imaging further narrows the focus to localized penile pathology.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: The ulceroproliferative lesion's morphology, associated penile edema, and lymphadenopathy raised suspicion for malignancy (e.g., invasive squamous cell carcinoma), supported by the patient's age and symptoms (burning micturition, weight loss).
Step 2: Histopathology ruled out invasive penile cancer, showing changes confined to the epithelium without malignancy in deeper tissues.
Step 3: The absence of fever, discharge, or systemic signs reduced the likelihood of infectious causes such as syphilis, balanitis, or fungal infections.
Step 4: Clinical appearance and histopathological findings excluded other penile dermatoses like lichen planus and psoriasis of the penile skin.
Step 5: A wedge biopsy identified penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3, a high-grade precancerous condition.
Step 6: Histopathology of excised tissue confirmed carcinoma in situ with negative surgical margins, ruling out invasive carcinoma.
Step 7: Final diagnosis: Penile intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3, a high-grade precancerous epithelial lesion confined to the outer layer of the skin with an intact basement membrane.
